summary: O'Neil argues that breaking up big banks is not a technical problem but a political one. She outlines numerous policy mechanisms that could reduce bank size, from progressive capital requirements and trading restrictions to outright nationalization. After nearly five years attending Alt Banking meetings, she concludes that the real obstacle isn't a lack of viable plans but a lack of political will to implement them. She defends Bernie Sanders against criticism that his bank breakup plans lack specifics, arguing that political will is the crucial missing ingredient.
key_points: Multiple policy mechanisms exist to shrink big banks: progressive capital requirements, size limits, trading restrictions, reserve requirements, Glass-Steagall revival, or nationalization
The problem is not a shortage of plans but a shortage of political will to implement any of them
Banks could be given incentives to shrink themselves or simply be legally prohibited from exceeding certain sizes
Criticisms of Sanders for lacking specific bank breakup plans miss the point—political will matters more than technical details
Once political will exists, experts who study these issues can work out the implementation details
money_quotes: There are lots of ways to break up the big banks.
Progressive taxes that max out at 100% profit when the bank is as big as Bank of America.
We could give banks 4 years to get compliant.
We could go the other way entirely, and nationalize one of the big banks, or all of them, and let them remain big but treat them like utilities, and make them super duper boring.
What we do have a shortage of, though, is political will.
When I hear people complaining about Sanders not have a specific enough plan for breaking up the big banks, I think it's hogwash.
What Sanders has, which is sorely needed, in fact is the crucial missing ingredient to any discussion around big banks, is the political will to do something.
Once the political will is there, the details can be sorted out by people who think about this stuff all the time.
key_insight: The barrier to breaking up big banks isn't a lack of policy options—it's the absence of political will to pursue any of them.
